title: Financing the Gig Economy
abstract: Unlike traditional firm production, gig economy workers provide their own physical capital. As a consequence, the low-income households for whom gig economy opportunities are most valuable often borrow to participate. In the context of ride share, difference-in-difference analysis reveals increased vehicle purchases, borrowing, utilization, and employment around entry, but financially constrained individuals cannot participate. To assess the equilibrium importance of financing, I build and estimate a structural model of the gig economy. Access to finance proves critical for the gig economy's growth: without finance, equilibrium quantities would be 40% lower and prices 90% higher, and only higher-income households could participate as drivers.

article.introduction:
Firms like Uber, Lyft, and Airbnbhave created convenient markets for households to sell capital services with their durable consumption goods. Car owners can provide rides, and homeowners can house guests in their spare rooms. As these technologies have spread, individuals have joined the so-called gig economy in droves as drivers, delivery people, and hosts. As of 2019, Uber estimates that roughly 3 million Uber drivers accounted for roughly $30 billion in North American gross bookings. Airbnb hosts listed more than 7 million vacancies.1While there has been intense debate about the role of technology and regulatory arbitrage in the gig economy's growth, this paper focuses on a key distinguishing characteristic of gig economy firms. Unlike traditional forms of production, gig economy workers provide and finance their own physical capital.2
  This paper studies the trade-offs in organizing production around workers that supply their own capital. The key benefit is that households can use one asset for two purposes: durable consumption and capital income. This stands in contrast to traditional firms, whose capital goods provide only capital income, but comes at the cost of requiring that households finance the capital. Ride-share driving pays a low wage, so drivers come from the lower end of the skill and income distributions.3Low-income individuals often lack vehicles that meet ride-share standards and must therefore finance the acquisition of a vehicle to participate as drivers. However, these low-income households are often constrained from borrowing on the extensive margin or face high rates on the intensive margin.
  With both reduced-form evidence and a structural model, I show that over the period that ride share expanded in the United States, the financial system facilitated the necessary physical capital acquisition. The reduced-form evidence highlights the importance of finance in a partial equilibrium setting, showing that financially constrained individuals were unable to take up ride-share driving. The equilibrium structural model further allows to me assess how financing impacted gig economy outcomes inaggregate. I find that ride-share entry led to large welfare gains of roughly $25 billion annually among potential drivers and roughly $30 billion annually among riders, and that nearly all driver welfare gains concentrated among finance-reliant individuals in the lowest income quartile. While on the margin, financially constrained individuals were unable to take up ride-share driving, their aggregate effect was small: counterfactually eliminating the need for (but not the availability of) financing by endowing all individuals with sufficient liquidity to purchase cars with cash leads to ride quantity and welfare increases on the order of less than 1%. In stark contrast, had financing been unavailable because, for example, the cost was prohibitively high, the gig economy would have been significantly smaller: Ride quantities would have dropped by 40%, prices would have increased by 90%, and nearly all low-income drivers would have been excluded from the market, replaced by higher-income individuals who relied less on finance. Therefore, finance was both important and well allocated in the context of ride share.
  I first highlight a mismatch in ex ante capital ownership that leads to a reallocation after ride-share entry. Using a difference-in-difference analysis that exploits the differential timing of ride-share entry across cities in the United States between 2010 and 2016, I find that entry leads to a 1.6% increase in new auto sales, a 0.60% increase in employment, and among low-income individuals with ride-share-eligible vehicles, an additional 2,000 miles driven per year. These effects are large relative to the total number of ride-share drivers, which is roughly 0.9% of the employed population in mature markets. Interestingly, however, there is no change in ownership or utilization among people unlikely to be ride-share drivers, suggesting that utilizing a ride-share service is not a perfect substitute for owning one's own car. The results are largest in low-income ZIP codes, consistent with ride-share driving being most attractive to low-income individuals. The increases occur only among cars that meet the specific physical criteria set forth by Uber and Lyft.
  Crucially, this growth relies on financing. The same difference-in-difference approach shows that auto loan originations increase by 1%, implying that the majority of new auto sales are financed. To show that financial constraints mute the impact of ride-share entry, I exploit a provision of the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) that requires credit agencies to remove bankruptcy history 10 years after filing. Consumers who recently had their bankruptcy history purged follow the same difference-in-difference response to ride-share entry. In contrast, those who have not yet had the information removed do not respond at all. At the ZIP code level, areas that historically relied on banks for auto credit see smaller responses in terms of auto loans, sales, and employment growth because bank-dominated areas saw credit contractions following postcrisis regulatory changes.4Similarly, areas with large mortgage delinquency rates during the financial crisis saw smaller real effects, as residents had difficulty obtaining credit when ride-share entered.
  The reduced-form facts show a significant reallocation of capital toward low-income drivers who rely on access to consumer finance. These findings, while well-identified, correspond only toindividualsin a partial equilibrium setting. My primary objective, however, is to examine how access to financein aggregateimpacted prices, quantities, welfare, and participation across the income distribution. The reduced-form analysis is not well suited for this purpose. For example, if large numbers of low-income individuals lose access to financing, earnings for ride-share drivers may adjust upward and bring in greater numbers of higher-income drivers. The counterfactual necessary to evaluate these forces does not exist in the reduced-form setting and instead requires taking a structural stand on the supply of and demand for ride-share services that incorporates trade-offs around labor supply, car purchasing, and car financing decisions.
  To get around the shortcomings of the reduced-form analysis, I build a structural model in the spirit of Berry, Levinsohn, and Pakes (1995) (BLP), more recently applied in a consumer finance context by Egan, Hortaçsu, and Matvos (2017), Benetton (2021), and Di Maggio, Egan, and Franzoni (2022). Specifically, I extend these models to explicitly capture the consumer financing and ride-share labor market decisions that lie at the heart of my analysis. The parameters in the model are identified using some of the reduced-form facts discussed above. I use other reduced-form findings to verify the model by examining the model's predictions for untargeted moments. I then use the estimated structural parameters to run equilibrium counterfactuals around access to finance and alternate systems of organizing ownership and capital utilization.
  I first examine the role of finance in gig economy growth by comparing the baseline model to a model in which finance is unnecessary but still available by endowing all consumers with sufficient liquidity to buy cars with cash if desired. This counterfactual addresses the narrow question of whether, in effect, U.S. consumer financial markets channeled reasonably priced financing to potential ride-share drivers. The model answers in the affirmative. While on the margin some borrowers are constrained, for example, those with adverse credit reports discussed above, their impact is quantitatively small: ride-share quantities and aggregate welfare would increase on the order of 1%, with a correspondingly small drop in prices. The largest impact is on those in the lowest income quartile, who continue to derive only a modest benefit.
  The effect of making finance unavailable by making financing costs prohibitively high is more stark. In the status quo, ride-share driving is dominated by finance-reliant, low-income drivers. Without finance, these drivers are forced to exit the market. Ride-share prices then increase, inducing higher-income, less finance-reliant drivers to enter. In aggregate, ride prices increase by 90% and quantities decrease by 40%. Nearly all welfare gains that accrued to low-income individuals in the status quo disappear, with quantitatively smaller gains instead accruing to middle-income individuals who can obtain cars without finance. The lesson from this counterfactual is therefore that the financial system was largely effective in allocating physical capital, as without it the gig economy would be dramatically smaller and operate to the benefit of much higher-income individuals.
  Finally, I relax the constraint tying ownership to driving by giving car owners the added option of hiring minimum-wage drivers to use their cars. In aggregate, allowing this more flexible arrangement reduces ride-share prices by 12% and increases quantities by 7%, leading to aggregate welfare gains. The most dramatic impacts are distributional: whereas the lowest income quartile provides almost all gig economy capital under the status quo, without the constraint the capital supply shifts toward higher ends of the income distribution. As more high-income individuals supply cars, falling ride prices make lower-income owner-drivers worse off in which case they supply less capital and see smaller welfare gains from ride share. Thus, likely unintentionally, the technological limitation that workers own capital in the gig economy benefits low-income individuals.
  More generally, my model highlights and quantifies important costs and benefits in reorganizing production so that capital owners can use capital for both production and durable consumption. Beyond ride share, the findings in this paper help explain why other technologies that allow durable consumption goods to be used to produce capital income—such as Airbnb—have succeeded, while other seemingly similar technologies that do not—such as WeWork—have not succeeded. Finally, the paper highlights an important role that consumer finance plays in supporting growth and facilitating household production and the widespread adoption of disruptive technological innovations.
  Related literature. My paper connects questions and methodologies from household and corporate finance. Like recent work in household finance (e.g., Egan, Hortaçsu, and Matvos (2017), Benetton and Fantino (2021), Benetton (2021), Buchak et al. (2018a), and Di Maggio, Egan, and Franzoni (2022)), I use demand estimation techniques from industrial organization to study consumer behavior in financial markets. In contrast to these papers, I focus on a consumer who finances a capital good for production.5My paper extends traditional approaches by incorporating explicit financing and labor market choices which allow me to study the joint relationships between financing, employment, and productivity.
  More generally, studies of consumer finance typically focus on how the financial system and financial constraints impact household consumption, durable goods purchases, and savings (e.g., in Campbell (2006)), often in the context of cars (e.g., Grunewald et al. (2020), Benmelech, Meisenzahl, and Ramcharan (2017)), housing (Mian and Sufi (2011), Piskorski and Seru (2018), Hurst and Stafford (2004), Di Maggio et al. (2017)), and consumer loans (Zinman (2010), Melzer (2011)). My paper studies similar financial decisions but for the purpose of production. Similarly, while recent studies examine the connection between employment and consumer finance (e.g., in Dobbie et al. (2020), Donaldson, Piacentino, and Thakor (2019), and Herkenhoff, Phillips, and Cohen-Cole (2021)), my study examines the direct link between household financial access and the ability to work in a market that requires household capital. My findings complement literature evaluating the size and benefits of the financial system, such as Greenwood and Scharfstein (2013) and Philippon (2015), by highlighting another important role of consumer finance.
  My paper additionally speaks to a long line of research emphasizing the importance of the financial system for growth (e.g., as in Mian, Sufi, and Verner (2017), Kaplan and Zingales (1997), Jayaratne and Strahan (1996)) and finance for productivity (i.e., in remedying the sorts of misallocations as in Hsieh and Klenow (2009); see Lenzu and Manaresi (2018), Buera, Kaboski, and Shin (2011), and Midrigan and Xu (2014)). My paper contributes to this work by highlighting the importance of ex ante factor misallocation when a disruptive technological change is introduced that impacts how capital can be used. Here, the consumer finance system takes on a role typically played by corporate financial markets to reallocate factors of production toward more efficient users.
  Finally, in contrast to many papers that study the impact of ride share on consumer surplus, as in Cohen et al. (2016), Cramer and Krueger (2016), or Calder-Wang (2021) in the context of Airbnb; on labor markets, as in Hall, Horton, and Knoepfle (2017), Benjaafar et al. (2022), Cook et al. (2021), Cook, Diamond, and Oyer (2019), and Chen et al. (2019), or on traffic and vehicle crashes as in Barrios, Hochberg, and Yi (2023), I focus on the impact and implications of the capital stock, in particular, on the benefits of dual asset use and consumer finance. Other papers explore the theory behind this question. For instance, Filippas, Horton, and Zeckhauser (2020), Fraiberger and Sundararajan (2017), Razeghian and Weber (2016), and Ostrovsky and Schwarz (2018) consider theoretical models of vehicle ownership and utilization in a ride-share economy. I empirically evaluate the typical prediction that ride-share entry will lead to a smaller capital stock through greater utilization rates. I show empirically that this prediction is complicated by both the large option value that households obtain from car ownership beyond utilization, and the ex ante misallocation of capital.
  My paper proceeds as follows: SectionIdiscusses the data and methodology. SectionIIexamines the real effects of ride-share entry, while SectionIIIexamines the impact of finance. SectionIVdetails and estimates a structural model of ride-share financing, and SectionVdiscusses the results and concludes.
article.I. Data and Institutional Background:
Data: This section describes the data; SectionAof theInternet Appendixprovides greater detail and summary statistics.6I obtain the staggered entry dates of Uber and Lyft by hand-collecting company press releases and newspaper articles. I use a proprietary data set from Uber that provides the number of registered drivers at the core-based statistical area (CBSA)-month level. I obtain data on auto sales from R.L. Polk (now IHS Markit), on registrations from the North Carolina, Washington, and Indiana departments of motor vehicles (DMVs), and on auto loans from Equifax. R.L. Polk and Equifax provide nationwide data on the number of new vehicle sales and auto loans at the ZIP code level between 2010 and 2016. DMV data comprise vehicle-level registration data by ZIP and month, which merge with data from the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration and FuelEconomy.gov databases to obtain physical attributes of each car, including make, model, year, horsepower, and fuel efficiency. I obtain borrower-level data from a 10% sample of all borrowers in TransUnion, which provides information on an individual's borrowing activity and past bankruptcy filings. For the structural estimation, I obtain auto loan interest rates from RateWatch. I rely on Salary.com and Indeed.com to obtain the market-level income of ride-share drivers. Finally, I use publicly available government databases such as the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) Statistics of Income, the U.S. Census, and the American Community Survey (ACS) to obtain local demographic information.
  Institutional background: Uber began operations in San Francisco in 2010, with Lyft following shortly thereafter. Both services expanded rapidly to other cities. By the end of 2016, there were nearly 800,000 registered Uber drivers.7Ride-share entry is not random, which presents an identification challenge if entry is correlated with local conditions in industries related to auto sales or loans. SectionBof theInternet Appendixprovides details on the timing and determinants of ride-share entry. In short, entry is more likely in large cities with high mobile broadband penetration, suggesting that these services entered areas with large potential markets. Vehicle ownership rates or access to finance do not predict entry. These findings hold in terms of both the extensive margin of entry and the timing of entry.
article.II. Real Effects of Ride-Share Entry:
This section studies the empirical effects of ride-share entry. I first study ride share's impact on sales, employment, and vehicle utilization. As a threshold matter, it is important to note that while the overall rate of vehicle ownership in the United States is high, ownership rates are heterogeneous across the income distribution, with households that earn below the full-time-equivalent Uber income of an Uber driver owning roughly 0.20 fewer cars per adult household member than households that earn at the median income. SectionCof theInternet Appendixdetails these facts.
  I begin by studying the impact of ride-share entry on log auto sales using data from R.L. Polk. TableI, Panel A shows the results. Column (1) shows a 1.6% increase in vehicle sales following ride-share entry. Column (2) presents the within-CBSA regression and shows that following ride-share entry, low-income ZIP codes see auto sales increase by roughly 2.6% while high-income ZIP codes see auto sales increase by 0.6%. These findings are robust to the inclusion of time fixed effects that vary with income group, which indicates that the results are not driven by time trends that vary between low- and high-income ZIP codes unrelated to ride-share entry.
  Figure1presents the event study results. Panel A depicts the results for new auto sales for all ZIP codes. The figure shows no pretrends. An increase in auto sales occurs around the time of entry and follows similar dynamics as the entry of drivers depicted inInternet AppendixFigure1, Panel C. Figure1, Panel C shows these results for low-income ZIP codes and finds stronger effects, while Panel E shows the results for high-income ZIP codes and find no effects, as expected.
  As a robustness check, I first exploit ride-share vehicle eligibility requirements and detailed vehicle-level registration data. To be eligible for ride share, a vehicle must be no older than 15 years, have four doors, and be a sedan, SUV, or minivan. In SectionDof theInternet Appendix, I show that increases in vehicle registrations correspond entirely to eligible vehicles in low-income ZIP codes.
  As an additional robustness check, I repeat the preceding analysis but split ZIP codes on 2010 transportation worker share rather than wage to confirm that the ZIP-level income measure captures variation relative to ride-sharedrivingin particular. The results, reported in column (3) of TableI, Panel A, show that increases in auto sales concentrate in ZIP codes with a high share of transportation workers.
  As a final robustness check, I run a placebo test of regressions (1) and (2), with the dates of ride-share entry are randomly assigned across locations. The results, reported in SectionLof theInternet Appendix, show no significant effect, confirming that the preceding findings are related to ride-share entry and not uncontrolled-for-time trends in the data.
  I next test whether ride-share entry coincides with increases in low-income employment by using data from the IRS Statistics of Income database. These data show the number of tax filings in the ZIP code and year by adjusted gross income (AGI) buckets. The specification follows (1) and (2) using the log number of tax filings as the outcome variable.8The outcome at the ZIP-AGI bucket-year level includes year-AGI and ZIP-AGI fixed effects, and the low-income differencing variable is an indicator for whether the AGI bucket is below $25,000 per year rather than a differencing variable at the ZIP code level. The $25,000 income level is the relevant threshold because full-time ride-share driving pays slightly below this level on average.
  TableII, Panel A, column (1) shows an increase in tax filings across all AGI brackets of roughly 0.6% following ride-share entry. Column (2), which includes the low-income AGI interaction, shows that the increase in filings concentrates entirely in filings in the ⩽$25,000 AGI bucket, leading to an employment increase of $1.1%. Column (3) includes ZIP× year fixed effects to absorb any time-ZIP-varying economic conditions correlated with the endogenous entry choice and shows that the differential effect between low- and high-AGI filings persists.
  To check for pretrends, Figure2, Panel A, presents the results of the event study for all filings aggregated at the ZIP-year level across buckets, together with 95% confidence intervals. The figure shows no pretrends. Once ride-share enters, tax filings increase. Panels B and C split the sample into filings below and above $25,000, respectively, and confirm that tax filings increase for low-income filers but not for higher-income filers, with no pretrends. As a robustness check, I use the fact that ride-share drivers report earnings as business rather than wage income, which the IRS data report separately. SectionFof theInternet Appendixconfirms that the increases are driven by increases in business tax filings. Finally, placebo tests reported in SectionLof theInternet Appendixshow no effect.
  I next test whether ride-share cars see higher utilization rates after entry. The South Carolina DMV data report vehicle odometer readings with vehicle ownership. I calculate yearly utilization rates for completed ownership spells and test whether utilization rates of ride-share-eligible cars change following ride-share entry in low-income ZIP codes. The specification again follows (1) and (2) but is run at the vehicle-ownership spell-ZIP level with interactions and fixed effects described below.
  TableII, Panel B, shows the results. Column (1), which shows the overall effect of ride-share entry, contains ZIP-eligibility and quarter-eligibility fixed effects. The effect is positive but insignificant, and implies that the typical ride-share driver drives roughly 140 miles per day.9Columns (2) to (6) include more interactions and fixed effects. Focusing on column (5), which includes the triple interaction of entry, the low-income ZIP indicator, and the vehicle eligibility indicator, the estimates show a large and statistically significant impact of ride-share entry on vehicle utilization in low-income ZIP codes among ride-share-eligible cars of roughly 2,000 miles per year. Placebo tests reported in SectionLof theInternet Appendixshow no effect.
  These positive changes are offset in aggregate by large decreases in utilization among ineligible vehicles and high-income ZIP codes. Importantly, while vehicle utilizationdecreaseson average in high-income ZIP codes, earlier results show that vehicle sales in high-income ZIP codes do not decrease, suggesting that vehicle utilization may not be an important factor in a consumer's decision to purchase a car for durable consumption. I return to this finding later.
article.III. Financing Ride-Share Growth:
This section explores how auto lending contributed to the real effects document above, and whether lack of access to finance on the extensive margin inhibits gig economy growth.
  Following the empirical strategy in regressions (1) and (2), I examine the impact of ride-share entry on log auto loan originations. The results in TableI, Panel B, mirror those for new auto sales. Column (1) shows a 1% increase in vehicle loans following ride-share entry. Column (2) reports results of the within-CBSA regression and shows that following ride-share entry, low-income ZIP codes see auto loans increase by roughly 2.1% while high-income ZIP codes see auto loans unchanged. These results indicate that the increases in physical car sales observed in earlier analysis are accompanied by significant increases in vehicle financing.
  Figure1presents the corresponding event studies. Panel B shows the results for auto loans for all ZIP codes. Like the results for sales, the figure shows no pretrends and an increase in loans around the time of entry. Panel D focuses on the low-income ZIP code subsample and finds stronger effects, while Panel F focuses on the high-income ZIP code subsample and finds no effect.
  I perform a battery of robustness checks analogous to those for auto sales. SectionDof theInternet Appendixexamines whether the registered vehicle has a lien, indicating that it is financed. The results show that the probability of having a lien increases following ride-share entry, but only for ride-share-eligible vehicles in low-income ZIP codes. I next split ZIP codes on 2010 transportation worker share. TableI, Panel B, column (3) shows that auto loan increases are concentrated in ZIP codes with a high share of transportation workers. Finally, I repeat the placebo tests above in SectionLof theInternet Appendixand find no significant effect.
  The previous results suggests a critical role for auto financing and hint that a lack of credit may dampen the real effects of ride-share entry. To examine financial constraints directly, I exploit the FCRA's requirement that credit agencies remove Chapter 7 bankruptcy filings from credit reports 10 years after filing. This policy generates exogenous variation in borrowing costs between individuals who have versus have not yet had this bankruptcy information removed. I test whether those individuals with higher borrowing costs borrow less in response to ride-share entry, and find that they do. I then bring these findings to the ZIP code level to test whether variation in credit access leads to smaller real effects. I generate ZIP-level credit access variation using (i) the share of consumer loans that became seriously delinquent in 2010 and (ii) the 2010 bank share of auto lending. Indeed, these measures predict less auto loan growth and lead to smaller real effects.
  TableIII, Panel B, presents the results, with coefficients shown in percentage terms. Column (1) is the overall level effect, which, while positive, is insignificant for this particular subsample of borrowers.10Column (2) includes the interaction between treatment and the bankruptcy flag indicator and shows a statistically significant impact of ride-share entry of roughly 24 bps for the unconstrained control group and a nearly zero effect for the constrained treatment group. The differential effect survives the inclusion of ZIP-time fixed effects in column (3). For robustness, columns (4) to (6) repeat this exercise using a narrower window, focusing on constrained borrowers that declared bankruptcy between 8.5 and 9 years prior to ride-share entry and unconstrained borrowers that declared bankruptcy between 11 and 11.5 years prior to ride-share entry. The estimates are unchanged.
  Figure3, Panels C and D show the cumulative difference-in-difference coefficients over the event window for the constrained and unconstrained groups, respectively. These figures show little change in origination probability for the constrained group, while the unconstrained group sees an increase in auto loans beginning on impact. The pretrends in both groups show no significant effects leading up to entry. This analysis shows that borrowers facing greater credit costs increase borrowing less in response to ride-share entry.
  I conclude the analysis by using ZIP-level variation in credit access to test whether financial constraints reduce the real effects of ride-share entry. I obtain variation from two sources. The first comes from the percentage of consumer loans that were in serious delinquency, defined as 60 days or more behind on payments, as of 2010. Consumers who were seriously delinquent on 2010 consumer loans will have impaired credit scores and therefore face higher borrowing costs when ride share enters. The second source of variation in credit availability comes from the 2010 bank share of auto lending. Following the financial crisis, new banking regulations such as increased capital requirements and stricter supervision reduced banks' ability to lend.11Those ZIP codes in which banks dominated auto lending in 2010 saw their lenders limited by regulations and faced a relatively constrained supply of auto credit.12
  Following the empirical strategy outlined above, I find that neither ZIP codes with a high share of delinquencies nor ZIP codes with a high share of bank credit supply see effects of ride-share entry in terms of auto loans, sales, and employment growth, demonstrating that access to credit is key for the real effects of ride-share entry to hold. The analysis shows that these ZIP codes demonstrate no pretrends, and placebo tests confirm that these differences are driven by the precise timing of ride-share entry. These results are shown and discussed in detail in SectionGof theInternet Appendix.
  To summarize, the results in this section establish a clear link between the supply of finance and ride-share uptake: low-income households require financing to obtain cars to drive in the gig economy. When they cannot obtain financing, gig economy growth and employment are reduced.
article.IV. A Structural Model of Ride-Share Fin:
Ride-share entry permits households to use their durable consumption goods to earn capital income. However, for households to obtain the necessary capital, they often need financing, and a lack of household access to finance becomes an impediment to gig economy participation. The previous section highlighted this trade-off in reduced form for financially constrainedindividualsin a partial equilibrium setting. While well-identified, a shortcoming of the analysis is that it does not permit an equilibrium analysis of the impact of finance at the aggregate level. In particular, individuals with adverse credit records may be excluded from gig economy participation, but the analysis is silent on the equilibrium effects of access to finance more broadly. For example,how manydrivers rely on finance, and should they lose access, would other, perhaps higher-income drivers, be willing to enter at a higher wage?
  To facilitate equilibrium analysis, I build a structural model. The model allows me to speak to the aggregate equilibrium effects of limiting access to finance on outcomes such as ride quantities, prices, welfare, and substitution patterns across the income distribution. I additionally use the model to examine the welfare and distributional impacts of a key technological limitation of gig economy production, namely, that workers must own the capital. I quantify the aggregate impact of this constraint and the subgroups that may benefit from it.
  Central to the model is the supply of and demand for ride-share services at the CBSA-year level. The supply side extends discrete choice models such as BLP, by adding financing and ride-share driving decisions to an individual's vehicle ownership decision. This generates a ride-share supply curve that jointly depends on—as shown in the reduced-form analysis—vehicle sales, financing, and individuals' outside-option wages. I estimate the model using many of the reduced-form facts established in the previous section and use untargeted moments to validate the model. The demand side uses a simple model of consumer ride-share demand calibrated with existing estimates from the literature. Equilibrium outcomes are pinned down by the intersection of supply and demand, which I now describe.
  A ride-share marketm∈{1,⋯,M}$m \in \lbrace 1,\dots ,M\rbrace$is characterized by a distribution of individual demographics,Fm(Di)$F_m(D_i)$, andϕm∈{0,1}$\phi _m \in \lbrace 0,1\rbrace$, an indicator for ride-share presence. For individuali∈{1,⋯,I}$i \in \lbrace 1,\dots ,I\rbrace$,Di$D_i$includes, for instance,i's outside-option wage. Individuali's demographicsDi$D_i$map to preferencesθi$\theta _i$. The mapping fromDi$D_i$toθi$\theta _i$is the key object to estimate.
  Individual preferencesθi$\theta _i$include, for example, utility from owning a car, wage and rate sensitivity, available liquidity and financing costs, and nonmonetary preferences over ride-share driving. These preferences jointly determine the individual's three decisions: (i) whether to acquire a vehicle, (ii) whether to finance the car or pay cash outright, and (ii) whether to become a ride-share driver. I describe these decisions below.
  Individualiobtains utility from car ownership for durable consumption, utility from how the vehicle is financed, and utility from using the car for income-producing activities. I describe the indirect utility of each component below:
  The individual is endowed with liquidityli∈θi$l_i \in \theta _i$. With sufficient liquidity,li≥0$l_i\ge 0$, the individual chooses between financing or a cash offer. With insufficient liquidity,li<0$l_i<0$, the individualmustobtain financing if she wants a car.
  Optimal choice: An individual's total indirect utility from car ownership is the sum of their utility from their own consumption, financing, and income-producing activities with the car. The timing of shock realizations are as follows. The individual takes the equilibrium ride-share pricepm$p_m$as given, and the consumption shockεic$\epsilon ^c_i$realizes. She then chooses whether to own a car taking into account her expected utility from financing and income-producing activities. After her ownership choice, the financing and income-producing shocks are realized and she decides how to finance the car and whether to become a transportation worker or ride-share driver, or whether to not use the car to earn income.17I characterize the solution to the financing and income-producing discrete choice problems before characterizing the antecedent purchase decision.
  Welfare: The producer surplus from ride-share entry (excluding ride-share platforms) is calculated as the aggregate change in ex ante welfare from the supply side of the model after introducing ride share,∫iEu(pm,θi,rm,1)−Eu(·,θi,rm,0)di$\int _i Eu(p_m,\theta _i,r_{m},1)-Eu(\cdot ,\theta _i,r_{m},0)di$. Dividing this quantity by individual price sensitivityαi$\alpha _i$expresses it in dollar terms. Consumer surplus comes directly from the demand equation (20). The counterfactuals quantify welfare from drivers' and riders' perspectives but omit potential externalities such as increased congestion or pollution.
  Estimation strategy and identification: The model generates many moments that map closely to empirical analogs described in the reduced-form section. This leads to a generalized method of moments (GMM) estimation strategy that I detail below. As described earlier, the key object to estimate is the mapping between demographicsDi$D_i$and individual preferencesθi={βic,fi0,αif,li,αi,γiR,γiT}$\theta _i = \lbrace \beta ^{c}_i,f^0_i,\alpha ^f_i,l_i,\alpha _i,\gamma ^R_i,\gamma ^T_i \rbrace$. Becauseθi$\theta _i$has seven elements, there are seven means plus seven×d$\times d$demographic coefficients to estimate, along with the variances of the T1EV shocks.
  Motivated by the reduced-form evidence, I make the following structural assumptions to reduce the number of parameters to be estimated. First, I assume that the only relevant demographic characteristic is outside-option income. Second, I assume that onlyβic$\beta ^c_i$,li$l_i$, andfi0$f^0_i$vary directly with income. Thus, the outside-option wage directly impacts preferences only through the wage impact on the consumption value of car ownership, access to liquidity, and the presence of financial constraints. Note that the outside-option wage also impacts the choice to take up ride share or other transportation work by acting as an opportunity cost for these activities. Finally, I normalize the variance ofεic$\epsilon ^c_i$to one.
  This leaves 12 parameters to be estimated: seven means,θ¯≡{β¯c,f¯0,α¯f,l¯,α¯,γ¯R,γ¯T}$\bar{\theta }\equiv \lbrace \bar{\beta }^c,\bar{f}^0,\bar{\alpha }^f,\bar{l},\bar{\alpha },\bar{\gamma }^R,\bar{\gamma }^T \rbrace$, three loadings on the outside-option wage,Π≡{πβc,πl,πf0}$\Pi \equiv \lbrace \pi ^{\beta ^c},\pi ^l,\pi ^{f^0} \rbrace$, and two variances,Σ≡{σF,σI}$\Sigma \equiv \lbrace \sigma ^F,\sigma ^I\rbrace$. These parameters are estimated by minimizing the distance from moments in the model to their empirical analogs. While a ride-share market is a CBSA-year, I exploit more granular geographic data at the ZIP-year level, analogous to the reduced-form moments explored earlier. Equilibrium in a market (CBSA) is calculated by aggregating the underlying ZIP codes. In addition to the reduced-form data, which I aggregate to the year-ZIP level, I additionally use a single cross-section of CBSA-level data on ride-share incomes forwR(pm)$w^R(p_m)$when estimating the supply side of the model.Internet AppendixTableXformally calculates the sensitivity of the targeted moments to changes in the estimated parameters in the spirit of Andrews, Gentzkow, and Shapiro (2017) by showing the percentage change in the targeted moment given a 1% increase in the parameter value. This analysis highlights that while the estimation identifies all parameters simultaneously, certain moments are particularly informative about key parameters, as I detail below.
  Vehicle sales: The model generates predicted vehicle sales at the ZIP-code level. The level of vehicle sales is informative aboutβ¯c$\bar{\beta }^c$: when own consumption utility increases, predicted sales increase. The parameterβ¯c$\bar{\beta }^c$is identified by matching model sales to observed sales. ZIP codes also vary in median incomes. The slope of this variation is informative aboutπβc$\pi ^{\beta ^c}$. A largerπβc$\pi ^{\beta ^c}$induces a stronger predicted relationship between ZIP code income and sales. Thus,πβc$\pi ^{\beta ^c}$is identified by matching how predicted sales vary with ZIP income.
  Financing shares: Financing shares informf¯0$\bar{f}^0$,α¯f$\bar{\alpha }^f$,l¯$\bar{l}$,πl$\pi ^l$, andπf0$\pi ^{f^0}$. To identify rate elasticity,α¯f$\bar{\alpha }^f$, I instrument for interest rates using deposit-weighted bank capitalization in the ZIP code. That is, ZIP codes in which banks have lower capital ratios (as determined by their market share of deposits) offer, on average, higher interest rates. The idea is that poorly capitalized banks face higher loan origination and servicing costs than better capitalized banks, and this acts as a cost shifter to local auto loan interest rates. SectionHof theInternet Appendixshows the construction and first stage for this instrument.
  Sales versus financing: The distinction between liquidityli$l_i$and nonrate financial costsfi0$f^0_i$is that while higherli$l_i$induces fewer loans and more cars, higherfi0$f^0_i$induces fewer loans and fewer cars. Hence, the relationship between sales and loans per sale informsf¯0$\bar{f}^0$andl¯$\bar{l}$. The relative differences in their slopes with respect to income informπl$\pi ^{l}$andπf0$\pi ^{f^0}$.
  Transportation worker and Uber driver share: Transportation worker shares pre-ride-share entry, together with quantities of ride-share drivers post entry, informα¯$\bar{\alpha }$,γ¯T$\bar{\gamma }^T$, andγ¯R$\bar{\gamma }^R$. I instrument for transportation worker wages using the fraction of workers that walk or bike to work as of 2000. ZIP codes with a higher share of walkers or bikers have lower demand for transportation services because more vehicles are required to provide these services. This demand shifter traces out the supply curve for transportation workers, identifying transportation worker labor supply elasticity,α¯$\bar{\alpha }$. Nonmonetary preferences over transportation work and ride-share driving,γ¯T$\bar{\gamma }^T$andγ¯R$\bar{\gamma }^R$, are informed by the residual worker quantities left unexplained by wage differentials.
  Finally, T1EV shock variances for financing and income producing activities are identified by minimizing market-level residuals of predicted financing and transportation work. Broadly, smaller shock variances lead to predictions that depend more on explicitly modeled utilities. Shock variances that minimize market-by-market residuals maximize how much variation the explicit model features capture.
  Estimated parameters: TableIVreports the estimated model parameters, with Panel A showing the estimated parameters and their bootstrapped standard errors, and Panel B showing parameters from the literature. The model hits the targeted moments, which are shown in SectionIof theInternet Appendix, fairly closely. The estimation produces parameter estimates that are consistent with the literature. Interest rate elasticity is estimated at 2.39, which similar to other estimates of interest rate elasticities in the consumer finance context.20Labor supply elasticity is 2.10, which is in line with analogous estimates of industry-specific or firm-specific elasticities.21
  The model estimates the dollar value of vehicle ownership, after financing costs, to be roughly $30,000, in line with car prices.22The value of vehicle ownership is strongly increasing with household income, consistent with the idea that higher-income households are more willing to pay for cars. In terms of financial constraints, the model finds that roughly 40%23of car ownersrequirefinancing (i.e., lack cash liquidity to buy without financing), but that higher-income households are less likely to do so. Finally, the nonmonetary value of working for ride-share over another transportation job is roughly $5,400.24
  Model validation: To further validate the model, I examine its fit of untargeted moments by replicating the reduced-form difference-in-difference experiment detailed earlier in the paper. In particular, I examine the change in vehicle sales and loans after ride share entry, both overall and by income level.25TableIVshows the difference-in-difference coefficients for the data and model. Across these untargeted moments, the model gives predictions that are both qualitatively and quantitatively similar to those observed in the data. Both data and model show an increase in log sales and loans of roughly 5% after entry excluding the entry year. As in the data, these effects are larger in low-income-quartile ZIP codes than in high-income-quartile ZIP codes.
  With the estimated model, I run two counterfactuals. First, I compare the baseline size, pricing, and participation rates across the income spectrum of the gig economy in two counterfactual financing environments: one in which financing is unnecessary but still available, and one in which no financing is available. While the reduced-form evidence in SectionIII.Bhighlights the importantpartialequilibrium role that access to finance played for individually constrained drivers, it cannot speak to the market-level impacts of finance on the growth of the gig economy, its welfare effects, and potential substitution of unconstrained drivers across the income distribution into gig economy work.
  Second, I examine the impact of an alternate ownership structure that allows car owners to rent their vehicles to other drivers for use in the gig economy. This counterfactual allows me to examine in sharp relief the aggregate consequences of requiring gig economy workers to provide their own capital, and highlights who in the income distribution benefits and who is harmed by this technological limitation.
  I compare two financing scenarios to the baseline estimation. In thefinancing not neededscenario, I endow all individuals with sufficient liquidity to make financing unnecessary, although it remains an option. In the model, I letl¯→∞$\bar{l}\rightarrow \infty$. This counterfactual can be interpreted as examining whether the financial system as estimated was able to provide capital where it was needed, or alternatively as the introduction of vehicle loans made available to all potential drivers at their individual discount rates.
  Thefinancing not availablescenario takes the other extreme by making financing unavailable. The model operationalizes this scenario by lettingf¯0→∞$\bar{f}_0 \rightarrow \infty$. Here, sufficiently liquid individuals pay with cash, while insufficiently liquid individuals will be unable to obtain cars. Conceptually, both changes lead to shifts in the supply of rides, with thefinancing not neededscenario representing a shift outward and thefinancing not availablescenario representing a shift inward.
  Figures4, Panels A and B show the quantity and price responses, respectively, to these shifts in the supply curve. When finance is not needed, quantities increase by a modest 6,000 drivers relative to the baseline of 770,000, and prices fall by only $0.25 per hour, a 1% decrease relative to baseline. In contrast, when finance is not available, quantities drop by more than 300,000 drivers, or 40% relative to baseline, and prices rise by $21 hour, which is nearly a 90% increase.
  The welfare consequences to drivers and riders are shown in Panels C and D, respectively. As a baseline, ride share's introduction leads to roughly a $25 billion annual driver surplus and a $30 billion annual rider surplus. In keeping with the modest effects on price and quantities, these surpluses are largely unchanged when financing is not needed. In contrast, when financing is not available, driver surplus from ride share falls to $18 billion per year, a 28% decrease. Similarly, rider surplus falls to $18 million, representing a 40% decrease relative to the baseline.
  Figure5examines the distributional impacts. Panel A shows the implied average outside-option wage of drivers across the three scenarios. In the baseline scenario and when finance is not needed, outside-option wages are roughly $13,500 per year, a $6.5 per hour full-time equivalent.26In contrast, the outside-option wage when financing is not available jumps to $48,000 per year, nearly $23 per hour.
  This effect works through financing. As Panel B shows, in the baseline case nearly all drivers use financing. When it is merely optional, only 70% use it, suggesting to a first approximation that 30% of driversrequirefinancing and the remainderpreferit. Making financing unavailable immediately excludes that 30% and makes the remaining 70% worse off. As these drivers cannot or choose not to obtain cars, the distribution of drivers shifts toward higher-income drivers, who as the estimation shows are less finance-reliant. However, because they have better outside options for work, equilibrium prices must increase to attract them.
  Panel C shows these dynamics. In the baseline scenario, driving skews heavily toward the lowest income quartile, with only a small number in the second quartile participating and essentially no higher-income individuals participating. When financing is not needed, there is a slight shift toward lower-income drivers, as obviating the need for financing allows the fairly small number of truly financially constrained low-income drivers to enter. These drivers correspond to the relatively small number of individuals affected by things such as the bankruptcy flag explored earlier. When financing is unavailable, the finance-reliant lowest income quartile exits the market. As prices increase, less finance-reliant drivers from the middle two quartiles enter. The effects on welfare, shown in Panel D, are largely parallel. In the baseline case, ride-share entry primarily benefits the lowest income quartile. Restricting access to finance dramatically reduces their welfare and shifts the benefits up the income distribution.
  This counterfactual starkly illustrates two findings. First, the relatively modest effects observed when financing is not needed suggests that, at least at the time when the gig economy arose, capital markets were able to effectively channel productive capital to those who needed it. While there are individuals who appear to face financial constraints, shown in both the structural model and the reduced-form analysis, this counterfactual suggests that these numbers are quantitatively small in equilibrium. Second, the counterfactual shows thatwithoutconsumer access to finance, the gig economy would have been significantly smaller, and nearly all of its gains would have gone to higher-income, rather than lower-income, individuals. Thus, finance played a critical role in the growth of the gig economy.
  Finally, it is worth reiterating how the model's predictions differ from a more naive prediction based on the amount of baseline financing. The model suggests that nearly all ride-share drivers use finance. Naively assuming that absent finance, all drivers would be unable to participate would suggest that ride share wouldshut down. However, the model highlights two margins of substitution that allow the gig economy to continue (albeit at a much smaller scale) without finance. First, some drivers are able to substitute away from financing toward cash. Second, as wages rise, higher-income, less finance-reliant individuals can enter the gig economy without financing. The model quantifies these margins of substitution.
  Next, I consider an alternate ownership structure of gig economy capital. In particular, I divorce the technological constraint tying driving to ownership, and allow car owners the option to hire drivers at the market-level minimum wagew̲m$\underline{w}_m$and earn the residual, while also retaining the option to drive themselves. In particular, a car owner's utility from using the car in ride share becomesαi(wR(pm)−min{wi,w̲m})+γiR+εiR$\alpha _i (w^R(p_m) - \min \lbrace w_i,\underline{w}_m\rbrace ) + \gamma ^R_i + \epsilon ^R_i$rather thanαi(wR(pm)−w̲i)+γiR+εiR$\alpha _i (w^R(p_m) - \underline{w}_i) + \gamma ^R_i + \epsilon ^R_i$. Note that I keep the nonmonetary benefits of gig economy driving,γiR$\gamma ^R_i$, constant. This captures both direct utility impacts on the car owner, such as increased wear-and-tear, as well as any net compensating differential paid to the driver for the nonmonetary benefits of gig economy driving such as flexibility or safety concerns. Notice that holding prices fixed, all individuals are weakly better off, although as the results will show, this is not the case in equilibrium.
  This counterfactual generates a modest shift outward in the supply curve. Figure6, Panels A and B, show that ride quantities increase by 7% and prices fall by 12%. Panel D shows the corresponding modest increase in rider surplus. In contrast to these modest effects, much larger effects appear in capital owner surplus, with Panel C showing the surplus generated by ride share increasing by more than 100%.
  Figure7, which examines the distributional consequences, helps explain these large changes. Panel A shows the outside-option income of gig economy capital owners. In the baseline case, these owners also must be the drivers. This generates a natural complementarity between having a low outside-option wage and owning gig economy capital. In the frictionless car-share case, no such restriction exists. Rather, higher-income individuals, who derive greater consumption utility from car ownership and face lower financing costs, can put their productive capital to use without having to spend their own time providing services. Thus, Panels A and B show that gig economy capital owners have significantly higher income and are less likely to finance the vehicle in the frictionless car-share.
  Turning to impacts along the wage distribution, Panel C shows that while in the baseline case, gig economy capital ownership was concentrated in the lowest income quartile, in the frictionless car-share case, ownership is much more evenly distributed across the income distribution. The lower-income owners are still, on average, driving their own cars, while the higher-income owners are renting them to other low-income drivers. Because higher-income individuals are now supplying their cars to the gig economy, the price of ride share services declines, and thus fewer low-income owners enter ride-share driving in the first place.
  In terms of welfare, Panel D shows that while the welfare of most income groups increases in the frictionless car-share scenario, individuals in the lowest quartile are worse off due to this equilibrium price effect. This counterfactual illustrates the important distributional consequences of how gig economy production occurs. By connecting ownership to driving, the distributional benefits of gig economy production falls primarily to the lowest end of the income distribution. Disconnecting ownership and driving leads to welfare increases overall, but in equilibrium low-income individuals are made relatively worse off.
  Next, I evaluate the sensitivity of the counterfactuals to two reduced-form assumptions in the model: the demand for ride-share services and intermediaries' pricing behavior. In particular, (i) the model takes demand elasticities established in Cohen et al. (2016), and (ii) assumes fixed fees and commissions with values from Mishel (2018). In SectionJof theInternet Appendix, I recompute the counterfactuals under different assumptions for these values. The counterfactual results are not sensitive to the demand specification, with the exception of the calculation of rider welfare. The economic reason for this lack of sensitivity is that the counterfactuals I consider are changes to the ride supply curve. The shape of the demand curve impacts only how these supply-side shifts are allocated between prices and quantities, which impacts rider welfare but has only second-order effects on drivers. The results are also largely insensitive to intermediaries' pricing behavior. In particular, driver behavior and surplus are relatively unaffected by intermediary pricing decisions because they are largely passed through to the relatively price inelastic riders.
  Finally, in SectionKof theInternet Appendix, I examine two additional counterfactuals that focus on complementary explanations for ride share's growth: (i) that ride share's growth was aided by the fact that people like owning cars for their own consumption and incidentally choose to supply ride-share services, and (ii) ride share's growth was aided by a nonmonetary hedonic preference of workers to drive for ride share (due to, for example, flexible working conditions) over similar, less flexible jobs. I run these counterfactuals by varying the own-consumption value of car ownership,βc$\beta ^c$, around reasonable bounds suggested by the literature and by setting the nonmonetary benefits of ride share driving,γR$\gamma ^R$, equal to the (lower) nonmonetary benefits of the transportation job,γT$\gamma ^T$. The counterfactuals suggest that both factors contributed to ride share's growth, with ride-share prices and quantities varying roughly between 3% and 12% under various assumptions.
article.V. Discussion and Conclusion:
The gig economy allows consumers to use durable consumption goods for production. The ability to do so is a quantitatively meaningful benefit of the gig economy. However, this benefit comes at the cost of making access to consumer finance a critical element in gig economy production and participation. Access is especially critical for the low-income households that would like to become ride-share drivers but lack the liquidity necessary to obtain a car.
  When finance is available, auto sales, auto utilization, and employment increase following ride-share entry. Conversely, in the absence of finance, these real effects are smaller. These reduced-form facts motivate a structural model that quantifies the gig economy's reliance on finance as well as the impact of the technological constraint that drivers own their cars. I show that without access to finance, ride-share quantities would be 40% lower and prices 90% higher. Production—as well as the benefits of the gig economy—would then shift from low-income, finance-reliant individuals to higher-income individuals who do not need to finance their capital. Eliminating the technological driver-ownership constraint modestly increases aggregate welfare, but harms the lower-income individuals who otherwise benefit from the higher ride prices this constraint leads to.
  These results highlight a novel feature of the gig economy that enables consumer durables to flexibly be used as capital goods that applies more broadly than to ride share alone. This feature helps explain, for example, why Uber, Lyft, and Airbnb—all of which share this pattern of repurposing consumer durables for production—have successfully competed with traditional incumbents, while companies like WeWork have not. This structure, however, relies on access to consumer finance, and my model that the success of such firms was facilitated by the low-cost, easy-access financial environment in which they arose.
  Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
